user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
It comes from the delta hedging activity.
If MM is long vanna = vol up/delta up, vol down/delta down for him.
You can have vanna flip around the middle of a local long fly pattern, or short fly pattern. 
The difference is that in a long fly pattern MM has positive vanna convexity, 
while in a short fly patter, MM has negative vanna convexity.

In positive vanna convexity, you have to see vol down in order to break through the zero vanna, 
while in negative vanna convexity, you need vol up. 

The reason is bcs in a long fly pattern, the speed is shorter to the middle, so is the vanna (to keep it simple). 
So mechanically you will naturally see vol up towards the center from any direction. This is what the vanna also dictates. 
Bcs if you move towards the center, MM becomes shorter gamma. It is below the center, MM has short vanna = vol up/delta down, 
but above the center MM has long vanna = vol up/delta up.
So whenever the spot moves towards the center, vanna forces MMs to buy into the direction of the momentum, and this increases the vol.

comment.body:
However on the otherside of the flip, everything changes. 
Bcs if you had long vanna above, if spot moves down towards the center of the fly (assuming long fly), vol increases - delta increases, MMs need to sell more. But below the vanna flip, they become short vanna = vol up /delta down, so suddenly they start to face delta crush, so they buyback hedges.

comment.body:
I said, vol increases mechanically.
So thats the natural, algorhytmic behavior of vol in a positive vanna convexity towards the flip.
In order to suppress vol a bit, you need idiosyncratic customer flow that suppresses it. 
Aligning btw with vol selling interest.

comment.body:
Have you read my post on Kelly? 
First, I decide how conviced I am, what porbability I assign to the trade, and what probability the Street assigns to the bet.
Then I check the positioning, bcs I don't want to be stopped out only if the momentum wanted to revert from lower. 
So risk taking is changing, but it is like 10% on avg for me on a single intraday trade. And if I miss, I take the loss, and revise my call. If still see that, I will take the position later. 
But I don't force it. 
The key is that I take position only if I actually got a read on the tape. 
No lotto and yolo shit

comment.body: Currently, I'm tracking vol changes by comparing the current ATM IV with previous days' IV in the same srike. What DTE do you think is appropriate? I'm using 30-ish now, with previous days' vol skews expiring on the same day. Does this approach seem correct?
comment.raw_body: Currently, I'm tracking vol changes by comparing the current ATM IV with previous days' IV in the same srike. What DTE do you think is appropriate? I'm using 30-ish now, with previous days' vol skews expiring on the same day. Does this approach seem correct?

comment.body:
On intraday trading I usually take 0DTEs.
But for 0DTE I never take OTM, thats suicide for directional long vol trades. OTM 0DTEs are good for hedging and certain vol strategies but not for spot trading.

I use slightly ITM contracts bcs of the slower theta decay, and ATMs when the conviction is high, bcs ATM has the most gamma, so it gains value the fastest.
I use 0DTE if I look for a very fast trade, like what you saw today morning. 
And I use 1-2DTEs if I expect a sustained momentum during the day, and here, if I see good convexity edge, I may take 22/25 delta OTMs too, but the key is that here the contract is 1+DTE
Mind theta risk!

comment.body:
When customers go complacent, they don't hedge the downside anymore, this is short skew basically. They underestimate downside risk. 
We kinda see that today too, but not net short skew, but less hedging activity relatively.
Usually I look for lvls where they are expected to panicking back to long the skew one by other. This can be roughly derived from the positioning, CBOE datas, like where is the net exposure, simulating VaR, or different vol patterns (via stochvol models) and realtime simulating the reaction of the positions.

comment.body:
Its not like a certain range, they move kinda random, and my model assigns probabilities to certain ranges. It has assumptions, bcs vanna is complex and kinda random. Don't forget that IV is the supply/demand itself, and you have different tenors too. 
The lvls, or sometimes ranges I give for vanna flip is the area where one can find the vanna flip with more than 90% probability. This is the idea

comment.body:
Listen,
The problem here that many plp in this group too thinks in extremes.
When you hear the word "bullish" you expect moon, when you hear the word "bearish" you expect bloodbath. This is the two-dimensionality I keep warning you all about.
I said bottom in January, we got that. I said to buy the dips on february, market wants up, BUT RISK IS HIGH! This means unstable flow, full of FOMO, and I kept emphasizing that it isn't good for longer-term trades, bcs it has no edge. It will go up, but with high risk = wide intraday fluctuations.
It went to ATH.
However on the OpEx week I kept warning you about that I'm not necessary bullish after OpEx, but lets wait for the dustle to set to see what the market wants. 
I explained these flows and my thoughts in the chat. 

I called the OpEx suppressive flows. As much as I know, Cem called upward OpEx... with his famous gif (selloff on SPXW expo, then buyback into Friday PM expo). I explained that it WON'T be supportive, I derived you that it will not able to break above key lvls and vol will increase into PM. 
Happened.
And after expo, I explained you that I see spot decay into March OpEx.
Slow spot decay, still no edge on longerterm trades, but on intraday trades and volatility. 
So the net trend is DOWN now, I just don't take like ES shorts. I only have a put jsut for sure, but nothing else. 
Don't understand the Q.
comment.raw_body:
Listen,
The problem here that many plp in this group too thinks in extremes.
When you hear the word "bullish" you expect moon, when you hear the word "bearish" you expect bloodbath. This is the two-dimensionality I keep warning you all about.
I said bottom in January, we got that. I said to buy the dips on february, market wants up, BUT RISK IS HIGH! This means unstable flow, full of FOMO, and I kept emphasizing that it isn't good for longer-term trades, bcs it has no edge. It will go up, but with high risk = wide intraday fluctuations.
It went to ATH.
However on the OpEx week I kept warning you about that I'm not necessary bullish after OpEx, but lets wait for the dustle to set to see what the market wants. 
I explained these flows and my thoughts in the chat. 

I called the OpEx suppressive flows. As much as I know, Cem called upward OpEx... with his famous gif (selloff on SPXW expo, then buyback into Friday PM expo). I explained that it WON'T be supportive, I derived you that it will not able to break above key lvls and vol will increase into PM. 
Happened.
And after expo, I explained you that I see spot decay into March OpEx.
Slow spot decay, still no edge on longerterm trades, but on intraday trades and volatility. 
So the net trend is DOWN now, I just don't take like ES shorts. I only have a put jsut for sure, but nothing else. 
Don't understand the Q.
